messages.content: Could you walk me through your typical workflow when examining a suspicious skin lesion? What critical parts of your examination go far beyond simply 'looking at a cropped photo' of the spot, which is typically what an AI does?

Honestly, it starts before I even look closely—I’m scanning the whole body as they walk in, noticing how they move, if they’re scratching something. When I get to the lesion, sure, I look—but I also *touch* it. Is it hard like a little pebble under the skin? Does it blanch when I press? That tells me more than any dermoscopic image alone. And I’m always comparing it to their other moles—context is everything. An AI showing me a cropped image of one spot with a "90% melanoma" score? Useless if it doesn’t know this patient has 60 identical-looking nevi and this one’s actually stable. Plus, I’m listening: “It started bleeding after my dog scratched it” changes everything. You can’t crop that out and expect me to trust a number.

top_results.label: Palpating for Induration
top_results.description: You have to press the lesion to feel if there's a hard lump underneath the pigment; a flat photo can't tell you if it's invading deeper tissue.

top_results.label: Tactile Surface & Inflammatory Signs
top_results.description: Running a gloved finger over the spot to feel if it's rough, scaly, or ulcerated, and checking for warmth or tenderness which changes the diagnosis immediately.

top_results.label: Phenotypic Pattern Matching
top_results.description: Does this mole look like it belongs on this specific patient, or is it an outsider compared to their other nevi and overall skin pattern?

top_results.label: Background skin normalization
top_results.description: How do you visually compare the lesion against the patient's surrounding skin tone and texture to establish a baseline?

top_results.label: Boundary definition beyond the lesion
top_results.description: What constitutes the critical visual boundary of interest that extends past the immediate edge of the suspicious spot?

top_results.label: Visual reveal through compression
top_results.description: How do you use pressure or stretching specifically to visualize subsurface structures (e.g., blanching) that are invisible at rest?

divergence.explanation: Major perspective difference: Expert focuses on 'Palpating for Induration' while you focus on 'Visual reveal through compression'. You need high-level bridging.

analogy.source_concept: hard like a little pebble under the skin
analogy.target_concept: feeling for firmness under the skin

structural_mapping.inputs: Surface of object (skin lesion or fruit skin)
structural_mapping.outputs: Tactile feedback (hardness or softness) vs Visual feedback (color change)

analogy.explanation: Just as you can't tell if a fruit is ripe just by looking at its skin, you can't tell if a lesion is hard just by looking at a photo—it requires touch. The AI sees the surface, but you feel the depth.

source_evidence.quote: hard like a little pebble under the skin

payload.scenario: When you said 'it's hard like a little pebble under the skin,' does that kind of tactile feedback ever show up in a cropped image, or is it something only your hands can detect?

jargon_terms.desc: A magnified, close-up image of a skin lesion taken with a special handheld device (dermoscope) that reveals details not visible to the naked eye.
Medical term for moles—common skin growths that are usually benign but can sometimes develop into melanoma.
When a skin lesion temporarily loses color or turns white when pressed, which can indicate certain types of benign conditions.
A serious type of skin cancer that can spread if not caught early; dermatologists are especially vigilant in identifying it.

messages.content: When visually diagnosing a complex skin condition, how much do you rely on non-visual factors—like the tactile feel of the lesion or the patient's verbal history—and how do you mentally weigh these against the visual evidence?

Honestly, the visual is just the starting point—it’s never the whole story. If a lesion feels firm or fixed to deeper tissue, that immediately raises my concern level, even if it looks benign on the surface. And if the patient says it’s been bleeding or changing fast, I’m already thinking malignancy before I even pick up the dermatoscope. The AI might see a symmetric pigmented patch, but I’m weighing texture, behavior, evolution—all things a static image can’t capture.

top_results.label: Palpating Induration
top_results.description: You know that feeling when you press down and it feels like a pebble under the skin? That hardness often screams malignancy even if the surface looks calm, and a camera just can't capture that density.

top_results.label: Symptom & History Context
top_results.description: Knowing if it itches like crazy, bleeds spontaneously, or flares after stress changes the diagnosis completely, but pixels don't itch or worry.

top_results.label: Lack of Depth Perception
top_results.description: AI analyzes surface pixels, but I need to know if this is flat or raised, fixed or mobile, because depth changes everything.

top_results.label: Quantifying Sensory Confidence
top_results.description: How do you mentally assign relative 'confidence scores' to tactile feedback versus visual patterns during diagnosis?

top_results.label: Dissonance Resolution
top_results.description: When patient history suggests malignancy but the image appears benign, how do you resolve this cognitive dissonance?

top_results.label: Visual Hierarchy for Non-Visual Data
top_results.description: How should an interface present non-visual data so it doesn't visually overwhelm the primary image but remains cognitively accessible?

divergence.explanation: Category-level difference within the same perspective. Expert: 'Palpating Induration', You: 'Quantifying Sensory Confidence'. Try connecting through shared workflow concepts.

probes.question: How does the movement of a skin lesion affect your risk assessment?
At what pressure level do you consider induration significant in diagnosis?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If a mass becomes more movable over time, would that change your assessment of 'Palpating Induration'?
If the hardness of a lesion only becomes apparent with deep pressure, would that change your assessment of 'Palpating Induration'?

extracted_quotes: the visual is just the starting point—it’s never the whole story
If a lesion feels firm or fixed to deeper tissue, that immediately raises my concern level
I’m weighing texture, behavior, evolution—all things a static image can’t capture

jargon_terms.desc: A handheld medical device that uses magnification and light to examine skin lesions in detail, helping dermatologists see structures not visible to the naked eye.
A sign that a lesion or growth is cancerous or has the potential to spread, as opposed to being benign (non-cancerous).
An abnormal area of skin, such as a rash, mole, sore, or bump, that may indicate disease.
